title: Cursor now has a mobile app for guiding your coding agent on the go
slides: Cursor launches mobile app for on-the-go agent control.
What coding can you actually trigger from your phone?
Not full IDE—just agent steering while you're away.
Start or pause tasks without opening your laptop.
Test it today: push a small fix from your phone.
thread_text:
1. Code from your couch?

Cursor's new mobile app lets you trigger real coding tasks without a laptop. No emulator—just agent steering.

2. Not a full IDE.

You’re not writing lines—you’re pointing the agent at a bug and saying go. The heavy lift happens on a remote machine.

3. Start or pause mid-commute.

Push a button to kick off a test suite or stop a deploy from your phone. No keyboard required.

4. The catch: trust the agent.

If you can’t review the code before it runs, who catches the mistake? The app assumes the agent knows the context.

5. Test it on a tiny fix today.

Pick a one-line bug in your repo, push the change from your phone. Does it feel like control or just delegation?
video_script: Cursor now runs on your phone.
Steer coding agents without a laptop.
It's not a full mobile IDE.
Test a small fix from your phone.

script: Cursor now runs on your phone.
Steer coding agents without a laptop.
It's not a full mobile IDE.
Test a small fix from your phone.

instagram.caption:
You can now kick off a coding task from your phone without opening your laptop. 🛋️

Cursor's new mobile app doesn't run a full IDE—it lets you steer an agent remotely. Start a test suite, pause a deploy, or trigger a small fix while you're away from your desk. The heavy code work happens on a remote machine.

For you, this means you can push a one-line bug fix from your phone. But the trade-off: you're trusting the agent to get the context right without reviewing the code first.

Pick a tiny bug in your repo right now—does tapping "push" feel like control, or are you just delegating? 🧐
